g.data-narration: Welcome to Memory Management Basics - essential foundation for understanding how operating systems manage computer memory efficiently.
The memory hierarchy shows different storage types arranged by speed - from fast registers to slow disk storage.
Why learn memory management? These concepts appear in exam numericals and help understand why modern systems use paging.
Address binding maps logical addresses to physical addresses at different times - compile time, load time, or execution time.
The CPU generates logical addresses which the Memory Management Unit translates to actual physical addresses in RAM.
Fixed partitions divide memory into static regions, causing internal fragmentation - wasted space within allocated partitions.
Variable partitions create dynamic-sized memory regions, reducing waste but causing external fragmentation from scattered free space.
Three strategies allocate memory: First Fit finds first hole, Best Fit uses smallest fit, Worst Fit uses largest hole. First Fit is generally preferred.
Internal fragmentation occurs when allocated memory is larger than needed - the wasted space sits inside the partition.
g.text: Internal Fragmentation = Partition Size - Process Size

g.data-narration: External fragmentation happens when free memory is scattered in small blocks - total free space is enough but no single block is large enough.
Three solutions combat fragmentation: Compaction shuffles memory, Paging eliminates external fragmentation, Segmentation provides logical division.

g.text: Shuffle memory to create large block

g.data-narration: Memory protection uses base and limit registers to ensure processes can only access their allocated memory region.
Let's recap the key concepts: Memory hierarchy, address binding stages, allocation strategies, and fragmentation types.
For exams: Remember First Fit is preferred, calculate fragmentation using formulas, and know the difference between internal and external fragmentation.

g.text: • First Fit calculation: Find first hole ≥ process size
• Internal = Partition - Process | External = Total free but not contiguous
